new_value: toArray() uses single result array, in-order traversal
dirty flag skips redundant mergeOverlaps, 3.2x improvement
O(n) balanced bulk build from sorted array
shared module-level scratch arrays for insert flags
O(1) size counter with insert/remove tracking
first()/last() O(log n) via min()/max() tree walk
Tested: no measurable performance difference. assert cost is negligible.
Low value: chopAll sort is O(n log n) on 1000 items (~50μs), negligible vs total. Pre-sorted callers get O(n) from Timsort anyway.

{"id":"intervaltree-g44","title":"model-check test (chopAll) fails: size mismatch between tree and oracle","description":"The model-based property test (modelCheck.test.ts) fails on the ChopAllCommand with 'expected 4 to deeply equal 3' — the IntervalTree chopAll() produces a different number of intervals than the ArrayIntervalCollection oracle after the same sequence of operations.

Reproduction: npm run model-check (seed: -238212978, path: '3')

The failure is pre-existing (confirmed by stashing all changes and re-running). The root cause appears to be a discrepancy between how IntervalTree.chopAll() handles bulk range subtraction vs the oracle's sequential chop() calls, specifically around duplicate interval handling or edge cases in the linear sweep algorithm (IntervalTree.ts lines 191-264).

Key files:
- src/IntervalTree.ts (chopAll method, lines 191-264)
- src/ArrayIntervalCollection.ts (chop method used as oracle)
- src/modelCheck.test.ts (ChopAllCommand class, line 193-213)

The chopAll fast-path (linear sweep) may produce different results than sequential chop() calls when intervals overlap and the addAll/add deduplication behavior differs between the two paths.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jbb@scryent.com","created_at":"2026-03-29T02:15:31Z","created_by":"Jordan Baker","updated_at":"2026-03-29T02:15:31Z"}
